reasoning: As of late October/early November 2025, macroeconomic indicators continue to point toward stubborn inflation, leading markets to price out a December 2025 rate cut and even factor in an ~18% chance of a rate hike in January 2026. This fundamentally hawkish near-term trajectory heavily suppresses the likelihood of an aggressive 50+ bps cut occurring just 2.5 months away at the late January 2026 meeting. A 50 bps cut would require a sudden, severe macroeconomic shock (such as a catastrophic jobs report or an acute financial crisis) over an extremely short time horizon. The base rate for such black swan events in a ~2.5 month window is around 3-5%. While Polymarket traded at 9% on October 30, this likely reflects typical prediction market overpricing of longshot tail events (longshot bias) or hedging demand. Therefore, I establish the true probability significantly lower than the somewhat inflated 9% anchor, at around 5%.
